Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation

Source: R/ptdiff_MM.R
ptdiff_MM.Rd

Calculates the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the difference between two independent non-standardised t-distributed random variables using the Moment-Matching (MM) approximation. Specifically, computes \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\) or \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\), where \(T_i \sim t(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2, \nu_i)\).

Usage

ptdiff_MM(q, mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2, nu.t1, nu.t2, lower.tail = TRUE)

Arguments

q

A numeric scalar representing the quantile threshold.

mu.t1

A numeric scalar or vector giving the location parameter of the first t-distribution.

mu.t2

A numeric scalar or vector giving the location parameter of the second t-distribution.

sd.t1

A positive numeric scalar or vector giving the scale parameter of the first t-distribution.

sd.t2

A positive numeric scalar or vector giving the scale parameter of the second t-distribution.

nu.t1

A numeric scalar giving the degrees of freedom of the first t-distribution. Must be greater than 4 for the MM approximation.

nu.t2

A numeric scalar giving the degrees of freedom of the second t-distribution. Must be greater than 4 for the MM approximation.

lower.tail

A logical scalar; if TRUE (default), the function returns \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\), otherwise \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\).

Value

A numeric scalar or vector in [0, 1]. When mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, or sd.t2 are vectors of length \(n\), a vector of length \(n\) is returned.

Details

The difference \(Z = T_1 - T_2\) is approximated by a single t-distribution \(Z \approx t(\mu^*, {\sigma^*}^2, \nu^*)\) whose parameters are determined by matching the first two even moments of \(Z\):

  • \(\mu^* = \mu_1 - \mu_2\)

  • \({\sigma^*}^2 = (\mathrm{Var}_1 + \mathrm{Var}_2)(\nu^* - 2)/\nu^*\), where \(\mathrm{Var}_i = \sigma_i^2 \nu_i / (\nu_i - 2)\).

  • \(\nu^*\) is obtained from the fourth-moment equation.

The approximation requires \(\nu_1 > 4\) and \(\nu_2 > 4\) for finite fourth moments. It is exact in the normal limit (\(\nu \to \infty\)) and works well in practice when \(\nu > 10\). Because it reduces to a single call to pt(), it is orders of magnitude faster than the numerical integration method (ptdiff_NI) and fully vectorised.

Examples

# P(T1 - T2 > 3) with equal parameters
ptdiff_MM(q = 3, mu.t1 = 2, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,
          nu.t1 = 17, nu.t2 = 17, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.248517

# P(T1 - T2 > 1) with unequal variances
ptdiff_MM(q = 1, mu.t1 = 5, mu.t2 = 3, sd.t1 = 2, sd.t2 = 1.5,
          nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.6483581

# P(T1 - T2 > 0) with different degrees of freedom
ptdiff_MM(q = 0, mu.t1 = 1, mu.t2 = 1, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,
          nu.t1 = 5, nu.t2 = 20, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.5

# Lower tail: P(T1 - T2 <= 2)
ptdiff_MM(q = 2, mu.t1 = 3, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1.5, sd.t2 = 1.2,
          nu.t1 = 12, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = TRUE)
#> [1] 0.3097574

# Vectorised usage
ptdiff_MM(q = 1, mu.t1 = c(2, 3, 4), mu.t2 = c(0, 1, 2),
          sd.t1 = c(1, 1.2, 1.5), sd.t2 = c(1, 1.1, 1.3),
          nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 10, lower.tail = FALSE)
#> [1] 0.7456908 0.7174810 0.6818673
